lesson.menu_desc: Reviewing Supply Chain Failures with Mixed Conditionals -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 8 -- What Should Have Happened -- Reviewing Supply Chain Failures
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Fabiana Michelly Silva | Aula 8 -- What Should Have Happened

listenings.text: Hi Fabiana, it is Klaus. Before the post-mortem this afternoon, here is the short version of what went wrong. A single supplier failed, and because that supplier was our only one, it became a single point of failure. If we had used dual-sourcing, we would not be staring at an empty line today. The stockout hit during a demand spike, so the fallout was huge. In hindsight, the warning signs were there for weeks. If our forecasting were sharper, we would have spotted the spike in time. We had no safety stock, only a thin stopgap that bought us two days. The ripple effect reached three regions. A full overhaul of the supplier list is on the table. So the post-mortem is not about blame. It is about foresight: what corrective action builds real resilience, and how we stop a recurrence. Come ready to be honest. Talk soon.

Hi team, here is my read after the post-mortem. It was honest, and that mattered. The root issue was a single point of failure: one supplier, no backup. If we had built safety stock last quarter, we would not be scrambling now. And if our process were any stronger, the line would not have stopped at all. In hindsight, the demand spike was predictable, so this is really a foresight problem. The fallout was a ripple effect across three regions, far beyond a quick stopgap. Our corrective action is clear: dual-sourcing on critical parts, a real safety buffer, and a full overhaul of how we forecast. That is what builds resilience and prevents a recurrence. If I had pushed for this a year ago, today would look very different. I will send the lessons-learned document by Friday.

extra_audio.text: A single supplier fails and the line stops completely. The stockout hits during a demand spike, and the fallout spreads across three regions. Klaus and Fabiana run an honest post-mortem to find the real cause. They agree the corrective action is dual-sourcing, safety stock, and a full overhaul. Fabiana writes the lessons-learned document to prevent any recurrence.
